_comment_file_overview: FILE: manifest.json (also called the 'App Descriptor')
PROJECT: ShopEasy - SAP UI5 Online Shopping Application
NAMESPACE: com.shopeasy.app
[BEGINNER] WHAT IS manifest.json?
This is the APPLICATION DESCRIPTOR — the single most important configuration
file in any UI5 application. It describes EVERYTHING about your app:
- What it's called and what it does
- What data sources it connects to
- What libraries it needs
- What models (data containers) it uses
- How navigation/routing works
- What the first screen (root view) is
Think of it as your app's 'resume' or 'passport' — it tells the UI5 runtime
and the SAP Fiori Launchpad everything they need to know about your app
WITHOUT having to look at any code.
[INTERMEDIATE] WHY NOT JUST CONFIGURE IN JAVASCRIPT?
Before manifest.json (introduced in UI5 1.30), all configuration was done
in Component.js using the 'metadata' property. The manifest.json approach
is better because:
1. It's declarative (describe WHAT, not HOW)
2. It can be read without executing JavaScript (better for tooling)
3. The SAP Fiori Launchpad can read it to show app tiles without loading the app
4. It separates configuration from logic (clean architecture)
[ADVANCED] THE THREE MAIN NAMESPACES
manifest.json is divided into three major sections (namespaces):
- sap.app:  Application-level metadata (id, version, data sources)
- sap.ui:   UI technology metadata (which UI framework, device support)
- sap.ui5:  UI5-specific configuration (models, routing, dependencies)
[GOTCHA] JSON DOES NOT SUPPORT COMMENTS!
That's why we use '_comment' fields throughout this file. These are ignored
by UI5 at runtime. In production, you might remove them to reduce file size.
Some teams use JSON5 or JSONC during development and strip comments in the build.
_comment_version: This tells UI5 which manifest features are available.
Version 1.65.0 corresponds to UI5 1.65. Higher versions unlock
newer manifest features (like flexible column layout configs).
If you use a feature from a newer version but declare an older

_comment_sap_app: [BEGINNER] This section describes your application's identity.
It answers: WHO is this app? WHAT does it do? WHERE does it get data?
If your app were a person, sap.app would be their ID card:
- dataSources = where they get their information
_comment_id: [BEGINNER] The UNIQUE IDENTIFIER for your application.
2. The data-name attribute on the component div in index.html
3. The namespace used in your Component.js, views, and controllers
This ensures global uniqueness, especially important on SAP BTP
(Business Technology Platform) where multiple apps coexist.
[ANTI-PATTERN] Don't use short ids like 'myapp' or 'shop'.
They'll collide with other apps in a Fiori Launchpad environment.
_comment_type: [BEGINNER] The type of this project. Values include:
- 'component': A reusable component (embedded in other apps)
sap.app.title: ShopEasy - Online Shopping
_comment_title: [BEGINNER] The human-readable name of your app.
This is displayed in the Fiori Launchpad tile, browser tab,
This references a key in your i18n.properties file, allowing
sap.app.description: A comprehensive online shopping application for learning SAP UI5 concepts
_comment_description: [BEGINNER] A longer description of what the app does.
Shown in app info and the Fiori Launchpad app finder.
Like title, this should use i18n in production: '{{appDescription}}'
_comment_applicationVersion: [BEGINNER] Your app's version number following Semantic Versioning:
[INTERMEDIATE] This version is independent of the UI5 framework version.
It tracks YOUR app's evolution. Update it with each release.

_comment_dataSources: DATA SOURCES — Where Your App Gets Its Data
[BEGINNER] dataSources define the backend services your app talks to.
Think of them as 'registered connections' to servers that provide data.
- A uri (the URL path to the service)
- Settings (version, local mock data path, etc.)
By declaring data sources in manifest.json (not in code), UI5 can:
1. Automatically create OData models from them
2. Swap the real service for mock data during development
3. Show data source info in the diagnostics tool (Ctrl+Alt+Shift+S)
4. Enable the SAP Fiori Launchpad to manage service connections
[ADVANCED] In SAP BTP / Cloud Foundry deployments, the actual service
URLs are often overridden by destination configurations, so the URI
here acts as a relative reference that gets resolved at runtime.

_comment_mainService: [BEGINNER] 'mainService' — Our Primary OData Service
uri: '/sap/opu/odata/sap/SHOP_SRV/'
The URL path to the OData service. In a real SAP system, this
would point to a backend service created in SAP Gateway (SEGW).
The '/sap/opu/odata/sap/' prefix is SAP's standard path for
OData services. 'SHOP_SRV' is the service name.
type: 'OData'
Tells UI5 this is an OData service (as opposed to JSON or XML).
settings.odataVersion: '2.0'
We're using OData v2 (the most common in SAP systems).
OData v4 is newer and has a different model class in UI5:
v2 → sap.ui.model.odata.v2.ODataModel
v4 → sap.ui.model.odata.v4.ODataModel
[GOTCHA] v2 and v4 have VERY different APIs. Code written for
v2 will NOT work with v4 and vice versa. Don't mix them up!
settings.localUri: 'localService/metadata.xml'
Path to a local copy of the OData service metadata. This is
used for local mock server development so you can build the
frontend without a running backend.
[INTERMEDIATE] WHAT IS OData?
OData (Open Data Protocol) is a REST-based protocol that SAP
heavily uses. It provides standardized CRUD operations:
- GET /Products          → Read all products
- GET /Products('P001')  → Read one product
- POST /Products         → Create a product
- PUT /Products('P001')  → Update a product
- DELETE /Products('P001') → Delete a product
UI5's ODataModel handles all of this automatically. You rarely
write raw HTTP requests — the model does it for you.

_comment_sap_ui: [BEGINNER] This section describes the UI technology used.
It's mostly informational but affects how the app is loaded
and which device types are supported.
_comment_technology: [BEGINNER] The UI framework being used. Always 'UI5' for UI5 apps.
Other SAP technologies might use 'Web Dynpro' or 'BSP' here,
_comment_deviceTypes: [BEGINNER] Declares which device types your app supports.
Setting all to true means your app should work on desktops,
[INTERMEDIATE] The Fiori Launchpad uses this to decide whether
to show the app tile on a particular device type. If phone is
false, phone users won't see this app in their launchpad.
[BEST PRACTICE] When using sap.m controls, your app is
automatically responsive. Set all to true and test on multiple
screen sizes. Only set false if you KNOW it won't work on
[ADVANCED] You can also check device type at runtime:
This is useful for conditional logic (e.g., show a master-detail
layout on desktop but a single list on phone).

_comment_sap_ui5: [BEGINNER] This is the BIGGEST and most important section.
It configures everything UI5-specific about your app:
This section is ONLY read by the UI5 runtime. The SAP Fiori
Launchpad primarily looks at sap.app and sap.ui.
_comment_rootView: [BEGINNER] rootView — THE FIRST VIEW YOUR APP DISPLAYS
This is like React's <App /> component — the top-level view
that wraps everything else. All other views are rendered INSIDE
this root view.
viewName: 'com.shopeasy.app.view.App'
The full namespace path to the view. UI5 resolves this to:
./view/App.view.xml
(because of our resourceroots mapping)
type: 'XML'
UI5 supports 4 view types: XML, JavaScript, JSON, and HTML.
[BEST PRACTICE] ALWAYS use XML views. They are:
- Declarative (easier to read and maintain)
- Supported by UI5 tooling (code completion, linting)
- Required for Fiori elements and flexible programming model
- Automatically optimized by the UI5 build tools
[ANTI-PATTERN] Don't use JavaScript views. They were popular
in early UI5 but are now considered legacy. XML views provide
better separation of concerns (view = XML, logic = JS controller).
id: 'app'
The id for this view instance. Combined with the component id,
the full id becomes: 'shopeasy--app'
async: true
Load the view asynchronously. Always set to true.
[GOTCHA] This must align with data-sap-ui-async in index.html.

_comment_dependencies: UI5 is modular. You don't load the entire framework — just the
libraries you need. This list tells UI5 which libraries to preload
so controls are available when your views need them.
[INTERMEDIATE] Each library provides a set of controls:
- sap.m: Mobile-optimized controls (Button, List, Page, etc.)
This is the MAIN library you'll use for almost everything.
- sap.ui.core: Core framework (required, provides base classes)
[ADVANCED] Libraries not listed here will be loaded on-demand when
a control from that library is first used. But preloading avoids
the delay when navigating to a view that uses those controls.
_comment_minUI5Version: [INTERMEDIATE] The MINIMUM UI5 version required to run this app.
If the runtime version is lower, a warning is logged.
This is for documentation/deployment — it doesn't actually block
the app from running on older versions (but things might break).
[BEST PRACTICE] Set this to the version you're developing with.

text files) and makes their values available in views.
The file would contain entries like:
For German translation, you'd create i18n_de.properties:
UI5 automatically loads the right file based on the user's
[BEST PRACTICE] ALWAYS use i18n for user-visible text.
Never hardcode strings in views or controllers.
[GOTCHA] The ResourceModel is ONE-WAY only. You cannot write
to it at runtime — it's read-only from the .properties files.

_comment_cart_model: [BEGINNER] THE CART MODEL — Local Client-Side Data
This JSONModel holds the shopping cart data entirely on the
client side (in the browser). It's NOT connected to any backend.
type: 'sap.ui.model.json.JSONModel'
JSONModel is the simplest model type. It holds a plain JavaScript
object and provides data binding to views. Think of it like
React's useState() but for the entire app.
settings.data: The initial data structure.
- items: []          → Array of products added to cart
- totalPrice: 0      → Running total of all items
- itemCount: 0       → Number of items in cart
- currency: 'USD'    → Currency for display
In views, you access cart data with the 'cart>' prefix:
{cart>/items}        → The items array
{cart>/totalPrice}   → The total price
{cart>/itemCount}    → Number of items
[INTERMEDIATE] JSONModel vs ODataModel:
JSONModel: Client-only, you manage the data manually.
Great for app state (cart, settings, UI state).
ODataModel: Connected to a server, CRUD operations are built-in.
Great for business data (products, orders, users).
[ADVANCED] JSONModel has a default size limit of 100 items
for list bindings. If your cart could have more than 100 items
(unlikely but possible), call:
oModel.setSizeLimit(500);

_comment_routing_overview: Routing is the system that controls which page/view the user sees
based on the URL. It's exactly like React Router or Vue Router.
URL: #/product/P001          → Show details for product P001
1. User navigates (clicks link, uses browser back/forward)
2. The URL hash changes (the part after #)
3. The Router matches the hash against defined route patterns
5. The target loads the corresponding view
6. The view is rendered in the rootView's designated area
[ADVANCED] UI5 uses HASH-BASED routing by default (#/path).
This means navigation doesn't cause full page reloads — it's a
Single Page Application (SPA). The hash is never sent to the server.

_comment_routes: [BEGINNER] ROUTES ARRAY — Mapping URLs to Views
- name: A unique identifier for the route (used in code to navigate)
- pattern: The URL pattern to match (after the #)
- target: Array of target names to display when this route matches
To navigate to a route from a controller, use:
This changes the URL to #/product/P001 and loads the productDetail target.
[GOTCHA] Route ORDER MATTERS! Routes are matched top-to-bottom.
Put more specific patterns before general ones. For example:

_comment_targets_overview: [BEGINNER] TARGETS — What to Display for Each Route
Targets define WHAT VIEW to load and WHERE to put it when a
route is matched. Each target corresponds to a view file.
The target name is used in the 'target' array of routes above.
When a route matches, it activates its targets, which load their
Because multiple routes can share the same target (reuse views),
and one route can activate multiple targets simultaneously
_comment_home_target: viewName: 'Home' → Combined with viewPath config, loads:
viewLevel: 1 → Determines the slide animation direction.
Lower number = 'further back' in the app hierarchy.
Navigating from level 1 to level 2 slides LEFT (forward).
Navigating from level 2 to level 1 slides RIGHT (backward).
This gives users a natural sense of navigation depth.
_comment_productList_target: viewLevel: 2 — One level deeper than Home.
Navigating Home(1) → ProductList(2) slides left (going deeper).
Navigating ProductList(2) → Home(1) slides right (going back).
_comment_cart_target: viewLevel: 2 — Same level as ProductList (both are 'second-level'
pages accessible from the Home page).
_comment_checkout_target: viewLevel: 3 — Deeper than Cart, representing the flow:

_comment_contentDensities: [BEGINNER] CONTENT DENSITIES — Touch vs. Mouse Optimization
UI5 has two 'density' modes that adjust the spacing and sizing
Tighter spacing, smaller touch targets. Optimized for mouse/keyboard.
Used on desktops where precise mouse clicks are possible.
More spacious, larger touch targets. Optimized for touch screens.
Used on tablets and phones where fingers need bigger tap areas.

Or in Component.js, auto-detect based on device:

and auto-detect at runtime. This ensures your app looks great on
_comment_resources: [BEGINNER] RESOURCES — Additional Files Your App Needs
css: Additional CSS files to load. The uri is relative to the
This is where you put custom styling that goes beyond what UI5
[BEST PRACTICE] Keep custom CSS to a MINIMUM. UI5 controls are
designed to be styled through themes, not custom CSS. If you find
yourself writing a lot of CSS, you might be fighting the framework.
[ANTI-PATTERN] Never directly style UI5 internal CSS classes
(like .sapMBtn or .sapMListItem). These are internal and can change
between UI5 versions, breaking your styling. Use 'addStyleClass()'
on controls and style your own class names instead.
